Lesson 3 of 3

ML monitoring and observability

Model drift detection, performance monitoring, alerting, and feedback loops for maintaining model quality in production.

25-35 min AI/ML flow MLOps and production AI 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Models degrade silently in production as data distributions shift. Without monitoring you discover failures from user complaints, not metrics.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. What to monitor Data drift: statistical divergence between training and serving distributions2. Drift detection methods Population Stability Index (PSI) measures feature distribution shift3. Feedback loops and retraining Automated retraining pipelines trigger on drift detection or schedule

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Models degrade silently in production as data distributions shift. Without monitoring you discover failures from user complaints, not metrics. 2. What to monitor Data drift: statistical divergence between training and serving distributions3. Drift detection methods Population Stability Index (PSI) measures feature distribution shift4. Feedback loops and retraining Automated retraining pipelines trigger on drift detection or schedule
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Models degrade silently in production as data distributions shift. Without monitoring you discover failures from user complaints, not metrics.

What to say first

  • Monitors both technical metrics (latency, errors) and ML-specific metrics (drift, accuracy).
  • Has automated alerting for significant distribution shifts.
  • Maintains feedback loops that connect monitoring to retraining decisions.
2

Study section

What to monitor

Input distributions, prediction distributions, latency, error rates, and business metrics collectively signal model health.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Data drift: statistical divergence between training and serving distributions
  • Concept drift: the relationship between inputs and correct outputs changes over time
  • Business metrics: revenue, conversion, satisfaction measure real-world impact
3

Study section

Drift detection methods

Statistical tests (KS test, PSI), embedding drift, and performance-based triggers identify when models need retraining.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Population Stability Index (PSI) measures feature distribution shift
  • Embedding drift detects semantic changes in unstructured input data
  • Performance monitoring requires labeled ground truth, often with delay

Code example

Detect data drift with PSI and KS test

import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

def population_stability_index(expected, actual, bins=10):
    """PSI > 0.2 indicates significant distribution shift."""
    breakpoints = np.linspace(min(expected.min(), actual.min()),
                              max(expected.max(), actual.max()), bins + 1)
    expected_pct = np.histogram(expected, breakpoints)[0] / len(expected)
    actual_pct = np.histogram(actual, breakpoints)[0] / len(actual)
    # Avoid log(0)
    expected_pct = np.clip(expected_pct, 1e-4, None)
    actual_pct = np.clip(actual_pct, 1e-4, None)
    psi = np.sum((actual_pct - expected_pct) * np.log(actual_pct / expected_pct))
    return psi

# Simulate drift: training vs production feature distributions
train_data = np.random.normal(0, 1, 10000)
prod_data = np.random.normal(0.5, 1.2, 10000)  # shifted!

psi = population_stability_index(train_data, prod_data)
ks_stat, ks_pval = stats.ks_2samp(train_data, prod_data)

print(f"PSI: {psi:.4f} ({'DRIFT' if psi > 0.2 else 'stable'})")
print(f"KS test: stat={ks_stat:.4f}, p={ks_pval:.2e}")
4

Study section

Feedback loops and retraining

Automated retraining triggers, human feedback collection, and A/B testing close the loop between monitoring and improvement.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Automated retraining pipelines trigger on drift detection or schedule
  • Human feedback (thumbs up/down, corrections) provides ground truth labels
  • Champion-challenger testing validates new models before full deployment

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Monitors both technical metrics (latency, errors) and ML-specific metrics (drift, accuracy).
  • Has automated alerting for significant distribution shifts.
  • Maintains feedback loops that connect monitoring to retraining decisions.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Only monitoring infrastructure health without tracking model prediction quality.
  • Setting drift thresholds too sensitively causing alert fatigue.
  • Retraining automatically without validating that new models improve performance.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • How would you detect that a fraud model has degraded due to new attack patterns?
  • Design a monitoring system for a recommendation engine serving millions of users.
  • What is the difference between data drift and concept drift? Give examples of each.

Keep the thread going

1 code example 1 design drill

What to master

Study this in your own words

  • Monitors both technical metrics (latency, errors) and ML-specific metrics (drift, accuracy).
  • Has automated alerting for significant distribution shifts.
  • Maintains feedback loops that connect monitoring to retraining decisions.

Production lens

Failure modes to call out

  • Only monitoring infrastructure health without tracking model prediction quality.
  • Setting drift thresholds too sensitively causing alert fatigue.
  • Retraining automatically without validating that new models improve performance.

Practice aloud

Questions worth rehearsing

  • How would you detect that a fraud model has degraded due to new attack patterns?
  • Design a monitoring system for a recommendation engine serving millions of users.
  • What is the difference between data drift and concept drift? Give examples of each.

Code example

Implement drift detection

intermediate coding

Build a drift detector that monitors feature distributions and alerts when training and serving distributions diverge significantly.

What to notice

  • PSI < 0.1: no drift, 0.1-0.2: moderate, >0.2: significant drift
  • Use np.histogram with the SAME bin edges for both distributions
  • Clip values to avoid log(0) in PSI calculation

Expected outcome

Normal data: (False, low PSI); Drifted data: (True, high PSI > 0.2)

Starter code 
import numpy as np
from typing import Dict, Tuple

class DriftDetector:
    def __init__(self, threshold: float = 0.1):
        self.threshold = threshold
        self.reference_stats: Dict[str, Dict] = {}

    def set_reference(self, feature_name: str, values: np.ndarray):
        """Store reference distribution statistics from training data."""
        # TODO: Store mean, std, and histogram for comparison
        pass

    def check_drift(self, feature_name: str, values: np.ndarray) -> Tuple[bool, float]:
        """Check if current distribution has drifted from reference.
        Returns (is_drifted, drift_score)."""
        # TODO: Compute PSI (Population Stability Index)
        # PSI = sum((actual% - expected%) * ln(actual% / expected%))
        pass

    def psi(self, expected: np.ndarray, actual: np.ndarray) -> float:
        """Compute Population Stability Index."""
        # TODO
        pass

# Test
detector = DriftDetector(threshold=0.2)
train_data = np.random.normal(50, 10, 1000)
detector.set_reference("age", train_data)

# No drift
serving_normal = np.random.normal(50, 10, 200)
print("Normal:", detector.check_drift("age", serving_normal))

# Significant drift
serving_drifted = np.random.normal(65, 15, 200)
print("Drifted:", detector.check_drift("age", serving_drifted))
Reference solution 
import numpy as np
from typing import Dict, Tuple

class DriftDetector:
    def __init__(self, threshold: float = 0.2):
        self.threshold = threshold
        self.reference_stats = {}

    def set_reference(self, feature_name: str, values: np.ndarray):
        hist, bin_edges = np.histogram(values, bins=10)
        self.reference_stats[feature_name] = {
            "hist": hist / hist.sum(),
            "bin_edges": bin_edges,
            "mean": values.mean(),
            "std": values.std()
        }

    def check_drift(self, feature_name: str, values: np.ndarray) -> Tuple[bool, float]:
        ref = self.reference_stats[feature_name]
        hist, _ = np.histogram(values, bins=ref["bin_edges"])
        actual_pct = hist / hist.sum()
        score = self.psi(ref["hist"], actual_pct)
        return (score > self.threshold, score)

    def psi(self, expected: np.ndarray, actual: np.ndarray) -> float:
        expected = np.clip(expected, 0.001, None)
        actual = np.clip(actual, 0.001, None)
        return float(np.sum((actual - expected) * np.log(actual / expected)))

Design drill

Design an ML monitoring dashboard

beginner

Design the key metrics and alerts for a production ML monitoring dashboard. Specify what to track, alert thresholds, and escalation procedures.

Use these prompts to deepen the study pass

  • What are the three tiers of metrics: infrastructure, model performance, business impact?
  • How do you set alert thresholds that minimize both false positives and missed issues?
  • What is the expected delay between drift occurring and it being detected?
  • How do you distinguish between data issues, model degradation, and concept drift?

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later.

0/3 saved Step 1/3: Explain the concept

PRACTICE GUIDE

Explain the concept Demonstrate deep understanding by explaining the topic clearly, covering intuition, algorithms, and practical applications.

Explain the concept

Explain "ML monitoring and observability" as if teaching a senior engineer who is new to AI/ML. Cover the core intuition, when and why you would use it, and the key algorithms or techniques involved.

Goal

Demonstrate deep understanding by explaining the topic clearly, covering intuition, algorithms, and practical applications.

Guardrails

  • How would you detect that a fraud model has degraded due to new attack patterns?
  • What to monitor
  • Drift detection methods
  • Monitors both technical metrics (latency, errors) and ML-specific metrics (drift, accuracy).

Suggested Structure

  • Core intuition
  • Key algorithms or techniques
  • When to apply
  • Common variants
